$comment:
Canonical-bytes worked examples for `Interlace-Sig`. Per wire/proxy-envelope.md §'Request canonicalization', the URL field is the cloister-router path observed at the proxy boundary (`/vault/proxy/<service>/...`), NOT the upstream URL. URL rewriting to upstream happens AFTER signature verification. Format: METHOD
URL
TS_MS
NONCE_B64URL_NO_PAD
BODY — single LF (0x0A) between fields; body may contain LF since it's the final field.

vectors.name: happy_bearer_chat_completion
vectors.description: POST /vault/proxy/openai/v1/chat/completions with a small JSON body. Same nonce as interlace-spec/0.1.0/test-vectors/lease-envelope.json:sample_envelope so cross-spec consumers see the structural symmetry. Body is the OpenAI chat-completion shape from injection-fixtures.json:openai_chat_completion.

vectors.name: happy_header_named_query_with_qs
vectors.description: GET /vault/proxy/github/repos/agentic-research/cloister?ref=main with an empty body. The query string IS part of the URL field, and IS part of the signed canonical bytes — a caller cannot strip ?ref=main between signing and the proxy. Headers don't appear in the canonical bytes (signature is over the request-line + URL + ts + nonce + body, not the header set).

vectors.name: happy_body_field_oauth_token
vectors.description: POST /vault/proxy/oauth-x/token with a JSON body — what the SKILL signs BEFORE the proxy injects client_secret. The signed canonical bytes are over the skill's body (without client_secret); the upstream body (with client_secret) is what injection-fixtures.json:oauth2_client_credentials produces. This separation is load-bearing: the caller cannot sign a body containing a credential it hasn't seen.
